risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Eligible and consented patients completed a baseline survey and were randomized to view the CRC screening decision aid or the control video before the physician encounter." Comment: The paper explicitly states that participants were randomized, and further notes that "randomization was stratified by site," which is a context clue consistent with a planned random allocation process rather than a non-random rule. No non-random allocation method is described anywhere in the report.

Summary: The report states that eligible and consented patients were "randomized to view the CRC screening decision aid or the control video" and that randomization was stratified by site, but it gives no explicit description of how allocation was concealed from the recruiters/enrolling staff before assignment. No central allocation, sealed opaque envelopes, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is described. Comment: Because the paper reports randomization but not concealment, the risk of foreknowledge of the next assignment cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "On the day of the physician visit, eligible and consented patients completed a baseline survey and were randomized to view the CRC screening decision aid or the control video before the physician encounter." and "research staff were not masked to the participants’ assigned study arm". Comment: Participants clearly knew whether they viewed the decision aid or control video, and the intervention was a behavioral educational/video program delivered before the encounter. Because the main outcomes were patient-reported communication, intention, and test preference, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence these outcomes; the lack of masking of staff further increases the chance of performance-related differences.

Quote: "Outcomes reported here reflect the effect of the decision aid part of the intervention on communication and decision-making outcomes assessed via survey directly after the provider encounter" and "All surveys were available in English and Spanish and were administered orally by a study team member; participants followed along and indicated their answer choice to the study team member". Comment: The outcomes were assessed by the participants themselves via interviewer-administered post-encounter surveys, so the patient was effectively the assessor for these patient-reported outcomes. The paper also states that "research staff were not masked to the participants’ assigned study arm," which could bias measurement. Because these outcomes (discussion, intent, preference, and knowledge survey responses) are subjective/self-reported and assessors were not blinded, detection bias is likely.

Quote: "A total of 267 participants were enrolled and randomized (134 intervention, 133 control)... Of these, 262 had complete data and were included in the analysis (Figure 1) (excluded were three intervention, two control)." Comment: Attrition was minimal overall (5/267, under 2%) and balanced between groups (3 vs 2). The paper also indicates a modified intention-to-treat approach excluding only those with no follow-up data, so missing outcome data are unlikely to have biased the results.
